articles.title: Valve releases Steam Controller CAD files under Creative Commons license
articles.title_display: Valve Releases Steam Controller CAD Files for Modding Under CC License
articles.summary:
## One-sentence takeaway  
Valve has open-sourced the complete CAD files for the Steam Controller under a Creative Commons license, empowering the community to study, modify, and build upon the hardware design.

## Body

### The Steam Controller’s Unique Place in Gaming Hardware  
When Valve launched the Steam Controller, it stood out in a crowded market of gamepads. Its design was unconventional, featuring dual trackpads instead of traditional thumbsticks, extensive customization options, and haptic feedback. This controller was crafted to bridge the gap between PC gaming and the living room, offering precision and flexibility for games not originally designed for controllers. However, despite its innovation, the Steam Controller never became a mainstream hit and was eventually discontinued.

### Why Release the CAD Files Now?  
Valve’s decision to release the Steam Controller’s CAD files under a Creative Commons license is a significant move. By doing so, they are not just sharing blueprints; they are inviting the community to engage with the hardware on a deeper level. This openness aligns with Valve’s history of supporting modding and community-driven innovation. It also reflects a growing trend in the tech industry toward transparency and collaborative development, especially for hardware that has a passionate user base.

### What’s Included in the Release?  
The released files are comprehensive. They include detailed 3D models of every component — from the shell and buttons to the internal mechanisms and PCB layouts. This level of detail allows engineers, hobbyists, and manufacturers to fully understand the controller’s construction and functionality. The files are compatible with popular CAD software, making them accessible to a wide range of users.

### Technical Insights and Design Choices  
Examining the CAD files reveals the thoughtful engineering behind the Steam Controller. For example, the dual trackpads are designed with precision sensors and haptic actuators to simulate the feel of a mouse. The ergonomic shape was sculpted to accommodate long gaming sessions, balancing comfort with accessibility to all controls. The internal layout optimizes space for batteries, wireless modules, and the custom chipset Valve developed for low-latency input.

### Trade-offs and Challenges in the Original Design  
The Steam Controller’s design was ambitious but came with trade-offs. The reliance on trackpads instead of thumbsticks required users to adapt, which was a barrier for some. The complex internal design increased manufacturing costs and posed challenges for repairability. By releasing the CAD files, Valve enables the community to explore alternative designs that might address these issues — for example, experimenting with different input mechanisms or simplifying the internal architecture for easier production.

### Potential Impact on the Community and Industry  
This release opens the door for a variety of projects. Makers can create custom mods or entirely new controllers inspired by Valve’s design. Educational institutions can use the files as a case study in hardware design and ergonomics. Small manufacturers might even produce derivative hardware without starting from scratch. More broadly, this move could inspire other companies to adopt similar openness, accelerating innovation in gaming peripherals.

### How to Access and Use the Files  
Valve has made the CAD files available through a public repository under a Creative Commons license, which permits sharing and adaptation with attribution. This choice balances openness with respect for Valve’s intellectual property. Engineers interested in the files can download them, inspect the detailed schematics, and even contribute improvements or new designs back to the community.

### Looking Forward: The Future of Open Hardware in Gaming  
Valve’s release of the Steam Controller CAD files is a milestone for open hardware in gaming. It demonstrates how a company can extend the life and influence of a product beyond commercial availability by empowering users and developers. As the gaming industry evolves, such transparency could become a key factor in fostering innovation, customization, and sustainability. For engineers and enthusiasts alike, this is an exciting opportunity to build on a unique piece of gaming history.

articles.title: CNN founder Ted Turner, a pioneer of cable TV news, dies at 87
articles.title_display: CNN founder Ted Turner, a pioneer of cable TV news, dies at 87
articles.summary:
## One-sentence takeaway  
Ted Turner, the visionary founder of CNN and a pioneer in cable TV news, revolutionized how the world consumes news by launching the first 24-hour news channel, profoundly shaping modern media before his passing at age 87.

## Body  

### The Birth of a New Era in News  
Ted Turner’s death at 87 marks the passing of a true media trailblazer. In 1980, Turner launched CNN (Cable News Network), the world’s first 24-hour cable news channel, fundamentally changing the news landscape. Before CNN, news was confined to scheduled broadcasts on major networks, limiting immediacy and depth. Turner’s vision was to provide continuous news coverage, accessible anytime, which was a radical departure from the norm.

### The Challenge of Building CNN  
Starting CNN was no small feat. Turner faced skepticism from established networks and advertisers who doubted the viability of a round-the-clock news channel. Cable TV itself was still growing, and the idea of dedicating an entire channel solely to news seemed risky. Turner invested heavily, both financially and personally, betting on the future of cable television and the appetite for nonstop news.

### Innovation and Impact  
CNN’s launch introduced several innovations. It was the first to provide live coverage of breaking news events as they unfolded, a capability that proved invaluable during crises like the Gulf War in 1991. CNN’s live broadcasts from the front lines brought viewers unprecedented immediacy and immersion. This approach set new standards for news reporting and forced traditional networks to adapt.

### Expanding the Media Empire  
Beyond CNN, Turner’s influence extended into other media ventures. He founded Turner Broadcasting System, acquiring channels like TNT and Cartoon Network, diversifying cable content. His business acumen helped grow cable TV into a dominant force in entertainment and information. Turner’s approach combined bold vision with savvy execution, enabling him to build a media empire that shaped American culture.

### Legacy and Influence  
Ted Turner’s legacy is immense. CNN’s model of 24/7 news coverage is now standard worldwide, influencing how news is produced and consumed in the digital age. His pioneering spirit helped democratize information access and accelerated the shift toward real-time journalism. Turner also championed philanthropy and environmental causes, reflecting a broader commitment beyond media.

### Why Turner’s Vision Mattered  
Turner’s gamble on continuous news coverage anticipated the modern demand for instant information. In today’s world of smartphones and social media, the expectation for immediate updates is ubiquitous. Turner’s early recognition of this need positioned CNN as a foundational pillar of global news infrastructure. His work underscores the importance of innovation in media to meet evolving audience behaviors.

### Final Thoughts  
Ted Turner’s passing closes a chapter on a transformative figure in media history. His creation of CNN not only disrupted traditional news but also expanded the possibilities of cable television. For engineers and technologists, his story is a reminder that bold ideas, combined with perseverance and strategic risk-taking, can redefine entire industries. Turner’s impact will continue to resonate in how we access and understand the world’s events.

articles.title: Live blog: Code w/ Claude 2026
articles.title_display: Anthropic’s Claude 2026: Multi-Agent Orchestration and Code Automation Updates
articles.summary:
## One-sentence takeaway

Anthropic’s Code w/ Claude 2026 event showcased how their AI platform is evolving less through new model releases and more by enhancing developer productivity, multi-agent orchestration, and practical tooling—scaling usage 17x year-over-year while enabling complex, long-running AI workflows and autonomous coding at enterprise scale.

## Body

### Setting the stage: no flashy new model, but big growth and product focus

The keynote at Anthropic’s Code w/ Claude 2026 event started with a familiar scene: no new Claude model announcement. Instead, the focus was on how Anthropic is improving the developer experience and product capabilities around their existing models. Ami Vora, Anthropic’s Chief Product Officer, emphasized real-world developer velocity stories from teams at Stripe and Binti, illustrating how Claude is already embedded in production workflows. The platform has seen a staggering 17x increase in API volume year-over-year, signaling strong adoption and scaling.

Rather than chasing the hype of new model releases, Anthropic is doubling down on making Claude’s products more usable and powerful. For example, they announced increased rate limits for Claude Code users, including a doubling of the five-hour usage limit for Pro, Max, and Enterprise customers. This capacity boost is backed by a partnership with SpaceX, leveraging their Colossus data center in Memphis—though the environmental impact of that facility has raised some concerns.

### From single models to multi-agent orchestration and long context workflows

A major theme of the event was moving beyond isolated model calls to more complex, coordinated AI workflows. Dianne Na Penn, Head of Product for Research, highlighted how Claude’s intelligence has matured to support tool use, long context windows, and adaptive thinking. The new Opus 4.7 model iteration powers these capabilities, with a particular strength in visual design and “infinite-feeling” context when combined with high-quality memory.

One of the most exciting product innovations is Claude Managed Agents, which enable multi-agent orchestration. This lets developers create fleets of agents that collaborate to solve complex tasks that a single model instance couldn’t handle alone. Anthropic introduced three new features here: multi-agent orchestration itself, “Outcomes” to define success criteria for iterative improvement (akin to a Ralph loop), and “Dreaming,” a research preview where Claude inspects past sessions overnight to identify gaps and self-improve. This approach supports workflows running for hours or even days, a big leap from last year when models could only work for minutes.

### Claude Code: evolving from CLI to full desktop GUI with autonomous coding

Claude Code, Anthropic’s AI-assisted coding product, also took center stage. Cat Wu, Head of Product for Claude Code, traced its evolution from a command-line interface to an IDE and now a full desktop app with rich previews and images. All these surfaces are built on the Claude Agent SDK, which external developers can also leverage.

A key pain point Claude Code addresses is reducing time spent on code review. They launched an automated Code Review feature used internally at Anthropic, plus “CI auto-fix” that automatically applies fixes to pull requests. These features aim to keep PRs clean so developers never see a red X, with Claude proactively prompting itself to fix issues.

Notably, Claude Code customers include Shopify and Mercado Libre, the latter boasting 23,000 engineers and targeting 90% autonomous coding by Q3 2026. This signals a shift where executives and managers are also getting hands-on with code again, empowered by AI to contribute without deep expertise.

### Demonstrations reveal asynchronous workflows and higher-order prompts

Boris Cherny, creator of Claude Code, gave a demo showing how the desktop app supports multiple asynchronous sessions, reflecting a future where much code is written asynchronously. He demonstrated Claude working on adding refunds to a dashboard, handling idempotency, multi-currency, and audit logging, while discovering edge-case bugs in real time.

A standout concept is “Routines,” described as higher-order prompts that enable developers to set up async automations. With Routines, developers can wake up to pull requests ready to merge, further streamlining the development lifecycle. This is a powerful abstraction that encapsulates repeated workflows and automates tedious tasks.

### Balancing speed, scale, and cost with advisor models and platform strategies

The event also touched on the trade-offs between speed, scale, and cost. The “advisor strategy” involves using the larger Opus model to provide advice on demand to smaller, cheaper models like Sonnet. This hybrid approach achieved better benchmark results at 5x lower cost for customers like eve, demonstrating how Anthropic is optimizing for practical deployment scenarios.

Claude Managed Agents aim to help teams ship 10x faster by bundling best practices like memory management out of the box. This reflects a broader trend of abstracting complexity so developers can focus on outcomes rather than infrastructure.

### Looking ahead: designing for future models and imaginative use cases

Throughout the keynote, a recurring piece of advice was to “design for the next model.” Developers are encouraged to build solutions that may not fully work today but will become viable as models improve. The teams getting the most from Claude are those focusing on automated evaluations, simple scaffolding, and creative applications that others haven’t explored yet.

This forward-looking mindset acknowledges that while current models are powerful, the real breakthroughs come from combining them with smart engineering and tooling. Anthropic’s investments in multi-agent orchestration, long context, and autonomous coding are laying the groundwork for these future capabilities.

### Conclusion: a maturing AI platform focused on developer productivity and real-world impact

Code w/ Claude 2026 was less about flashy new models and more about demonstrating how Anthropic is scaling AI usage through product innovation and developer enablement. The 17x API growth, SpaceX partnership, and enterprise adoption stories underscore Claude’s rising prominence.

By focusing on multi-agent orchestration, long-running workflows, autonomous coding, and cost-effective hybrid model strategies, Anthropic is positioning Claude as a practical platform for complex, real-world AI applications. The event’s demos and product updates reveal a thoughtful approach to balancing power, usability, and scalability—key ingredients for AI’s next phase in software engineering.

articles.title: Vibe coding and agentic engineering are getting closer than I'd like
articles.title_display: Simon Willison on the Blurring Lines Between Vibe Coding and Agentic Engineering
articles.summary:
## One-sentence takeaway

The line between casual "vibe coding" and professional "agentic engineering" is blurring as AI coding agents become more reliable, challenging traditional notions of code review, accountability, and software quality in production systems.

## Body

### Defining the divide: Vibe coding vs. agentic engineering

Simon Willison revisits a distinction he previously made between two emerging AI-assisted programming paradigms: vibe coding and agentic engineering. Vibe coding is characterized by a carefree, exploratory style where users—often non-programmers—ask AI to generate code without scrutinizing it, accepting bugs as a personal inconvenience. It’s a low-stakes, personal productivity hack. Agentic engineering, by contrast, is the responsible, professional use of AI tools by experienced engineers who maintain rigorous standards around security, maintainability, and performance. The goal here is to build high-quality, production-grade software faster, leveraging AI as a powerful amplifier of expertise.

### The unsettling convergence of two worlds

In a recent podcast conversation with Joseph Ruscio, Simon reveals a surprising realization: the boundary between vibe coding and agentic engineering is eroding in his own workflow. AI coding agents like Claude Code have become so reliable at generating well-structured, tested, and documented code that he no longer reviews every line for production systems. Instead, he treats these agents like trusted internal teams—black boxes whose outputs he consumes based on documentation and observed behavior rather than source inspection.

This shift feels uncomfortable because AI agents lack accountability and professional reputation. Unlike human teams, they cannot be held responsible for bugs or poor quality. Yet, their consistent correctness in routine tasks is fostering a creeping trust. Simon acknowledges the risk of the "normalization of deviance," where repeated success without oversight might lead to complacency and eventual failure.

### The new challenge: evaluating software quality in an AI era

Traditional heuristics for judging software quality—such as a well-maintained GitHub repo with many commits, comprehensive tests, and clear documentation—are losing their reliability. AI can generate polished repositories with all these hallmarks in minutes, making it impossible to distinguish between genuinely battle-tested projects and freshly minted AI outputs.

Simon values practical usage over superficial indicators. A project that has been actively used and exercised over weeks holds more credibility than one that looks perfect on paper but lacks real-world testing. This insight underscores a shift from static code quality metrics to dynamic, usage-based validation.

### Bottlenecks shift as code generation accelerates

With AI tools enabling engineers to produce thousands of lines of code per day instead of hundreds, the entire software development lifecycle is under pressure. Processes designed around slow, careful coding—like extensive upfront design phases—may no longer be optimal. Simon cites Jenny Wen’s talk from Anthropic, which argues that when the cost of building the wrong thing drops dramatically, design processes can afford to be riskier and more iterative.

This acceleration forces teams to rethink not just coding but also design, testing, deployment, and maintenance workflows to keep pace with the new velocity.

### Why experienced engineers still have an edge

Despite the rapid advances in AI coding, Simon is not worried about the obsolescence of professional software engineers. These tools amplify the capabilities of those who understand software deeply. The complexity and difficulty of producing robust software remain high, and AI is a force multiplier rather than a replacement.

He echoes Matthew Yglesias’s sentiment that while casual "vibe coding" may not appeal to everyone, professionally managed software companies using AI assistance to build better products is the future. Just as one wouldn’t DIY complex plumbing without expertise, relying on professional engineers augmented by AI remains the sensible approach.

### The enterprise perspective: trust through proven usage

Simon extends the idea of usage-based trust to enterprise software. He argues that no company wants to adopt a CRM or other critical system unless it has been proven in production by other large organizations over a significant period. This mirrors his personal preference for software that has been "vibe coded" and used daily for weeks.

This dynamic suggests that SaaS providers and enterprise vendors must demonstrate real-world reliability and adoption to compete against the temptation of bespoke AI-generated solutions.

### Conclusion: navigating a new software engineering landscape

Simon’s reflections highlight a pivotal moment in software engineering. AI coding agents are transforming how code is written, reviewed, and trusted. The neat categories of casual vibe coding and disciplined agentic engineering are merging, raising questions about accountability, quality assurance, and professional standards.

Engineers must adapt by embracing AI as a powerful collaborator while maintaining rigorous validation through usage and monitoring. Organizations will need to rethink processes and trust models to harness AI’s potential without sacrificing reliability. The future of software development is not AI replacing humans but humans working smarter with AI, navigating new complexities and opportunities together.

articles.title: Am I Meant To Be Impressed?
articles.title_display: Deep Dive: AI Revenue Illusions and Compute Dependency on OpenAI and Anthropic
articles.summary:
## One-sentence takeaway  
Despite massive capital expenditures exceeding $800 billion in 2026 alone, the leading tech giants’ AI revenue remains underwhelming, heavily reliant on a few outsized players like OpenAI and Anthropic, exposing a fragile and unsustainable AI growth narrative.

## Body

### The AI Spending Frenzy: Trillions Invested, But Where’s the Payoff?  
Over the past few years, the tech industry has poured unprecedented capital into AI, with Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and Meta expected to spend between $800 billion and $900 billion on AI-related capital expenditures in 2026, and over $1 trillion in 2027. This spending spree will push total AI capex beyond $2 trillion by the end of 2027. Yet, despite these staggering investments, the tangible returns are elusive. The narrative from these companies often touts AI as a growth engine, but the actual revenue figures tell a different story. Meta and Google, for example, either refuse to disclose AI-specific revenues or provide metrics so vague they are practically meaningless. This disconnect between investment and visible returns raises serious questions about the efficiency and sustainability of the current AI investment boom.

### The Illusion of Growth: Vague Metrics and Obfuscated Revenues  
Google CEO Sundar Pichai’s statements about AI “lighting up every part of the business” are emblematic of the broader issue: companies use optimistic language without backing it up with concrete revenue data. Google’s Gemini Enterprise, which reportedly grew 40% quarter-over-quarter, is a prime example of a metric that lacks context and transparency. Analysts and journalists often accept these claims at face value, amplifying the illusion of AI-driven growth without demanding hard numbers. Meta’s situation is even more telling. The company claims modest lifts in ad conversions attributed to its generative ads model, GEM, but the reported improvements—ranging from 1% to 5%—fail to translate into meaningful revenue impact. This pattern of vague, non-quantifiable AI benefits underscores a broader trend of hype overshadowing substance.

### Microsoft and Amazon: The Only Ones Sharing Numbers, But They’re Underwhelming  
Unlike Meta and Google, Microsoft and Amazon provide some AI revenue figures, but these numbers are far from impressive. Microsoft reported an AI revenue run rate of $37 billion (about $3.08 billion per month), while Amazon’s AI revenue run rate stands at $15 billion (approximately $1.25 billion per month). However, these revenues are dwarfed by their capital expenditures: Amazon’s AI revenue run rate is just 0.419% of its $298 billion AI capex to date, and Microsoft’s AI revenue run rate is only about 1.04% of its $293.8 billion AI capex. These ratios highlight a glaring inefficiency—massive spending with minimal revenue generation. Moreover, these revenues represent gross income, not profit, further emphasizing the underwhelming financial returns from these colossal investments.

### The OpenAI and Anthropic Dependency: A Fragile AI Revenue Backbone  
A critical insight is that much of Microsoft and Amazon’s AI revenue is not organic but heavily dependent on third-party AI companies they have invested in—OpenAI and Anthropic, respectively. OpenAI accounts for over 70% of Microsoft’s AI revenue run rate and consumes more than 80% of Microsoft’s AI GPU compute capacity. Similarly, Anthropic represents a significant portion of Amazon’s AI revenue. This dependency creates a precarious situation where the apparent AI growth of these tech giants is essentially a reflection of the fortunes of these outsized AI startups. OpenAI, for instance, is projected to lose over $25 billion in 2026, raising questions about the sustainability of this revenue model. This reliance on a few large AI players to drive revenue growth exposes the fragility of the broader AI ecosystem.

### Capital Expenditures Outpacing Revenue: A Losing Proposition?  
Microsoft’s AI revenue run rate of $37 billion in 2026, while seemingly large, is still less than the $37.5 billion it spent in capital expenditures in just the fourth quarter of 2025. This means that even at peak run rates, the company is not recouping its AI investments through revenue. The situation is similar for Amazon, where AI revenues are a tiny fraction of the capital spent. This disconnect between spending and revenue generation suggests that the current AI investment wave may be more of a speculative bubble than a sustainable growth driver. The massive capital outlays have yet to translate into profitable or even break-even AI businesses at scale.

### Why the Disparity? The Challenges of Monetizing AI at Scale  
The core issue lies in the difficulty of monetizing AI technologies at scale. AI infrastructure, particularly GPU compute, is extraordinarily expensive. The cost of training and running large models is immense, and the market for AI-powered products and services is still maturing. Many AI initiatives are experimental or in early stages, resulting in slow and uncertain revenue growth. Additionally, the hype cycle around AI encourages companies to invest heavily to avoid missing out, even if the immediate returns are unclear. This leads to a cycle of high spending with delayed or muted revenue realization. The reliance on a few dominant AI startups also concentrates risk and revenue, limiting the broader ecosystem’s ability to generate diverse and sustainable income streams.

### The Takeaway: Skepticism and Prudence Are Warranted  
The current AI investment landscape is marked by extraordinary capital expenditures, vague or disappointing revenue disclosures, and a heavy reliance on a few outsized players like OpenAI and Anthropic. While AI undoubtedly holds transformative potential, the financial realities reveal a more sobering picture. The tech giants’ AI revenues are small relative to their spending, and the growth narratives often rely on opaque metrics or third-party successes. For engineers, investors, and industry watchers, this calls for a healthy dose of skepticism. The AI boom is real, but the economic returns are far from guaranteed, and the hype may be masking fundamental challenges in turning AI investments into sustainable profits.

---

This story of AI investment and revenue underscores the importance of looking beyond headlines and optimistic statements. The trillion-dollar AI capex wave is reshaping the tech landscape, but it is also exposing the limits of current AI business models and the risks of overreliance on a few dominant players. The coming years will be critical in determining whether this massive investment translates into lasting value or becomes another tech bubble burst.

As AI coding agents become more reliable, the once-clear boundary between casual "vibe coding" and professional "agentic engineering" is blurring, raising new challenges around trust, responsibility, and software evaluation in production environments.

## Body

### The evolving landscape of AI-assisted programming

Simon Willison recently reflected on a conversation with Joseph Ruscio about AI coding tools, which sparked a deeper realization: the distinction between "vibe coding"—a casual, exploratory style of AI-assisted programming—and "agentic engineering," a disciplined, professional approach to building production-grade software, is becoming less clear-cut. Initially, Simon viewed vibe coding as something non-programmers might do without worrying about code quality, simply accepting whatever the AI produced and moving on if it worked. In contrast, agentic engineering involved experienced engineers carefully leveraging AI to build secure, maintainable, and performant systems.

### When vibe coding and agentic engineering start to overlap

Simon’s unease stems from how dependable AI coding agents like Claude Code have become. Tasks such as generating a JSON API endpoint with SQL queries, complete with tests and documentation, are now reliably handled by AI without his line-by-line review. This shift feels like treating AI-generated code as a semi-black box, similar to how engineers trust other teams’ services without inspecting every line of code. However, unlike human teams, AI agents lack accountability and professional reputation, which makes Simon question the responsibility of deploying their code in production without thorough review.

### The new challenge: evaluating software quality in an AI era

The traditional signals of software quality—commit history, detailed READMEs, comprehensive tests—are losing their reliability as indicators. AI can generate repositories with hundreds of commits, polished documentation, and full test coverage in minutes, making it impossible to distinguish between genuinely well-crafted projects and AI-generated facsimiles by inspection alone. Simon emphasizes that real value lies in actual usage and operational experience. A project that has been actively used and iterated on over weeks carries far more weight than freshly generated code, no matter how polished it appears.

### Shifting bottlenecks in the software development lifecycle

With AI dramatically increasing coding speed—from hundreds to thousands of lines per day—the traditional software development process is under strain. Processes designed to mitigate the risk of costly rework, such as extensive design phases, may become obsolete or need radical rethinking. Jenny Wen’s talk at Anthropic highlights this: since building software is now cheaper and faster, design processes can afford to be more experimental and risk-taking, reducing the overhead of getting the design "perfect" upfront.

### Why experienced engineers still have a role

Despite these advances, Simon is not worried about his career as a software engineer ending. He sees AI tools as amplifiers of expertise rather than replacements. The complexity and difficulty of producing robust software remain high, and AI cannot replace the nuanced judgment and deep understanding that experienced engineers bring. The analogy from political commentator Matthew Yglesias resonates here: just as most people prefer hiring professional plumbers rather than DIY plumbing, most users and organizations will prefer professionally managed software enhanced by AI assistance over unvetted AI-generated solutions.

### The enterprise perspective: trust through proven usage

Simon also touches on the enterprise angle, where risk tolerance is lower. Enterprises want proven solutions that have been battle-tested by other large organizations before adopting them. This mirrors the preference for software that has been actively used and refined over time, rather than freshly generated codebases. The implication is that SaaS providers and enterprise software vendors must focus on building trust through demonstrated reliability and operational history, not just on AI-generated feature velocity.

### Conclusion: navigating the blurred lines with care

The convergence of vibe coding and agentic engineering reflects the rapid maturation of AI coding tools. While these tools unlock unprecedented productivity gains, they also challenge long-standing norms around software quality, accountability, and trust. Engineers must adapt by developing new evaluation criteria centered on real-world usage and by maintaining professional rigor even as they delegate more tasks to AI. The future of software development will likely be a hybrid model where human expertise and AI capabilities complement each other, but the responsibility for quality and safety remains firmly human.

articles.title: Why not have changes in API behavior depend on the SDK you link against?
articles.title_display: Challenges of SDK-Dependent API Behavior in Windows
articles.summary:
## One-sentence takeaway  
API behavior should not depend on the version of the SDK your application links against because it leads to unpredictable, fragile, and hard-to-debug software that breaks the fundamental contract between an API and its consumers.

## Body

### The Problem: SDK-Dependent API Behavior  
In software development, an SDK (Software Development Kit) provides the headers, libraries, and tools developers use to build applications against an API. A tempting but flawed idea is to let the behavior of an API change depending on which version of the SDK your application was compiled with. At first glance, this might seem like a convenient way to introduce improvements or fixes without breaking existing applications. However, this approach creates a host of problems that undermine the reliability and maintainability of software.

### Why Changing API Behavior Based on SDK Version Is Tempting  
When an API evolves, developers want to fix bugs, improve performance, or add new features. If the runtime behavior could adapt based on the SDK version the application linked against, then new applications could benefit from improvements while old applications would continue to work as before. This might appear to be a smooth upgrade path without forcing all applications to recompile or change their code.

### The Reality: Fragility and Unpredictability  
In practice, making API behavior depend on the SDK version breaks the principle that an API is a contract between the library and the application. The contract should be stable and consistent regardless of how the application was built. When behavior changes based on the SDK, the same binary can behave differently depending on which headers and libraries it was linked against. This leads to several issues:

- **Debugging nightmares:** If a bug manifests only when linked with a certain SDK version, it becomes very hard to reproduce and diagnose.
- **Compatibility hazards:** Applications might unknowingly depend on subtle behaviors of a specific SDK version, causing regressions when the runtime or SDK changes.
- **Testing complexity:** Test coverage must account for all SDK versions, multiplying the effort and increasing the risk of missed edge cases.
- **Deployment confusion:** It becomes unclear what behavior an application will exhibit in production, since it depends on the build environment rather than just the runtime environment.

### The Right Approach: Versioned APIs and Explicit Contracts  
Instead of embedding SDK-dependent behavior into the API, the better practice is to version the API explicitly. This means:

- Defining clear, versioned interfaces or function sets.
- Maintaining backward compatibility by supporting older API versions alongside newer ones.
- Encouraging applications to explicitly opt into new behaviors by linking against or calling new API versions.
- Avoiding hidden behavior changes that depend on the build environment.

This approach preserves the contract between API and application, making behavior predictable and stable regardless of the SDK used at build time.

### Historical Lessons and Industry Practices  
The article draws on decades of experience from Windows development, where attempts to change API behavior based on SDK versions led to subtle bugs and compatibility issues. The Windows team learned that maintaining stable, versioned APIs with explicit upgrade paths is far more sustainable. This principle is echoed across the industry, where semantic versioning, feature flags, and explicit deprecation policies are preferred over implicit SDK-dependent behavior changes.

### Conclusion: Stability Over Convenience  
While it might seem convenient to have API behavior adapt to the SDK version, the long-term cost in reliability, debuggability, and developer trust is too high. APIs must provide a stable, predictable contract independent of the SDK version. By embracing explicit versioning and backward compatibility, software ecosystems remain robust and maintainable as they evolve.

articles.title: The bottleneck was never the code
articles.title_display: Coding Agents Shift Bottleneck from Code to Organizational Context
articles.summary:
## One-sentence takeaway  
The real bottleneck in software development is not writing code—it's producing clear, shared context and precise specifications that enable effective collaboration, especially as AI coding agents accelerate implementation.

## The myth of coding as the bottleneck  
For decades, software engineering has focused on making coding itself faster and easier. We’ve optimized typing speed, language design, frameworks, and tooling to reduce the friction of producing code. The assumption was that writing code was the slowest, hardest part of building software. But recent experiments with AI-powered coding agents, like those at .txt, challenge this view. These agents can generate code quickly and reliably when given good input. The bottleneck has shifted away from coding and toward something more subtle: the upstream work of deciding what code to write.

## Collaboration is the real work  
Software is fundamentally a collaborative activity. The code is just the residue of a complex negotiation among people about what the system should do. This insight isn’t new—Fred Brooks and Gerald Weinberg emphasized it decades ago—but it’s becoming clearer now that coding itself is cheap. The hard part remains aligning people’s understanding, managing trade-offs, and agreeing on precise requirements. Coding agents don’t solve this problem; they just expose it.

## Specifications become the new bottleneck  
With AI agents able to implement features rapidly, teams find themselves waiting not on code but on well-formed specifications. Managers and product teams must produce clear, testable acceptance criteria, detailed tickets, or precise designs that agents can execute. This shifts the bottleneck from engineers writing code to those defining what code should exist. In other words, management and product planning become the rate-limiting steps.

## The paradox of abundance and the need for focus  
When code becomes cheap to produce, teams tend to build more features, not fewer. This is a classic example of Jevons Paradox: increased efficiency leads to increased consumption. The temptation to ship every possible feature can dilute focus and overwhelm users. Steve Jobs famously emphasized that focus means saying no. In the era of coding agents, this discipline is even harder but more critical. Shipping more code faster doesn’t guarantee better products without ruthless prioritization.

## Context: the invisible currency of software teams  
All collaboration depends on shared context—the collective understanding of what the product is, why it matters, and how past decisions shape current work. Humans build this context through osmosis: conversations, shared experiences, and informal knowledge transfer. AI agents, however, lack this ambient context. They only know what is explicitly encoded in prompts, documentation, or code. Without rich, accessible context, agents produce plausible but imperfect outputs that miss the mark. Thus, context becomes the new scarce resource.

## Agents as context curators and knowledge base builders  
Ironically, while humans dislike producing explicit documentation, AI agents excel at reading and synthesizing large volumes of text: PR comments, issue threads, commit messages, and stale docs. At .txt, agents crawl the entire codebase and communication history to build a knowledge base capturing implicit decisions and conventions. This externalized context helps future agents and humans understand why things are the way they are. Creating this feedback loop—agents producing and consuming context—could raise the organizational IQ and reduce misunderstandings.

## The organizational moat in the age of AI coding  
The companies that thrive with AI coding agents won’t be those with the best models or infrastructure alone. Instead, success will come to organizations that master alignment and coherence at scale. They will be the ones who can maintain a shared understanding across hundreds or thousands of people while shipping more output per person. This is fundamentally a culture and management challenge, not a purely technical one. The new competitive advantage is organizational, rooted in how well teams communicate, focus, and manage context—not just how fast they write code.

---

In sum, the experiment at .txt and reflections on AI coding agents reveal a profound shift in software development’s bottleneck. The code itself is no longer the slowest part; the challenge lies in producing clear, precise, and shared specifications and context. As coding becomes cheaper and faster, the human work of negotiation, alignment, and focus becomes even more critical. AI agents can help by automating code generation and curating context, but they cannot replace the nuanced human work of deciding what to build and why. The future of software engineering lies in mastering this interplay between human collaboration and AI assistance.

articles.title: Asimov's three laws are merely a suggestion
articles.title_display: Asimov's Three Laws Are Ineffective as Hard Constraints for Modern AI
articles.summary:
## One-sentence takeaway  
Asimov’s Three Laws of Robotics, often treated as rigid ethical constraints, are in reality more of a loose guideline, and practical AI systems require nuanced, context-aware approaches to safety and control.

## Body

### The Myth of Asimov’s Three Laws  
Isaac Asimov’s Three Laws of Robotics—designed as a fictional framework to govern robot behavior—have long been cited as a foundational ethical guideline for AI and robotics. The laws state that a robot may not harm humans, must obey human orders unless they conflict with the first law, and must protect its own existence unless that conflicts with the first two laws. While elegant and memorable, these laws were never intended as strict, enforceable rules for real-world AI systems. The article argues that treating them as hard constraints oversimplifies the complex realities of AI safety and control.

### Why the Laws Are Merely Suggestions  
The core issue is that Asimov’s laws are inherently ambiguous and underspecified. They do not address the nuances of interpreting “harm,” the conflicts between competing human commands, or the robot’s understanding of context. Real-world AI systems operate in environments full of uncertainty, incomplete information, and conflicting objectives. The article highlights that blindly applying these laws can lead to unintended consequences or paralysis in decision-making. For example, a robot might refuse to act if it cannot conclusively determine whether an action might cause harm, or it might misinterpret ambiguous commands.

### The Challenge of Formalizing Safety in AI  
Attempts to formalize AI safety often run into the problem of translating high-level ethical principles into precise, verifiable rules. The article discusses how modern AI architectures, including large language models and autonomous agents, do not inherently possess a built-in ethical framework. Instead, safety is typically engineered through layered approaches: reward functions, constraint satisfaction, human-in-the-loop feedback, and rigorous testing. These methods acknowledge that no single set of rules—like Asimov’s laws—can cover every scenario.

### Trade-offs in AI Control Architectures  
The article explores different architectural choices in AI safety. One approach is rule-based systems that hard-code constraints, which can be brittle and fail in edge cases. Another is probabilistic or learning-based systems that adapt but may behave unpredictably. Hybrid models attempt to combine explicit rules with learned behaviors but face challenges in balancing flexibility and reliability. The key takeaway is that safety mechanisms must be designed with an understanding of trade-offs between strictness, interpretability, and adaptability.

### Practical Approaches Beyond Asimov  
In practice, AI developers focus on layered safety strategies rather than relying on a few overarching laws. These include continuous monitoring, anomaly detection, fail-safe mechanisms, and human oversight. The article emphasizes the importance of transparency and explainability so that AI decisions can be audited and corrected. It also notes that ethical AI requires ongoing iteration and context-specific tuning rather than a one-size-fits-all solution.

### Conclusion: Embracing Complexity in AI Ethics  
Ultimately, the article calls for a shift in mindset. Asimov’s Three Laws serve as a useful narrative device but fall short as a blueprint for real AI safety. Engineers and researchers must embrace the complexity of ethical AI design, recognizing that safety is a multifaceted challenge requiring diverse tools and constant vigilance. The “laws” are more of a starting point for discussion than a final answer, and practical AI systems demand nuanced, context-aware governance frameworks.

---

This perspective encourages experienced engineers to critically evaluate simplistic ethical models and invest in robust, adaptable safety architectures that reflect the messy realities of AI deployment.
