articles.title: The mythology of category theory
articles.title_display: Clarifying the Practical Limits of Category Theory
articles.summary:
## One-sentence takeaway  
Category theory’s reputation as an abstract, mystical discipline often overshadows its practical role as a unifying language and conceptual toolkit for structuring and reasoning about complex software systems.

## The mystique and misunderstanding of category theory  
Category theory has long carried a reputation bordering on the mythical within software engineering and mathematics circles. It’s often seen as an esoteric, highly abstract branch of mathematics, accessible only to a rarefied few. This mystique can intimidate engineers and lead to misunderstandings about what category theory actually offers. Rather than being a cryptic academic exercise, category theory provides a powerful, systematic way to describe and compose software abstractions. The article begins by addressing this mythology head-on, demystifying the subject and positioning it as a practical tool rather than an arcane art.

## Why category theory matters in software engineering  
At its core, category theory is about relationships and composition. It abstracts patterns of how things connect and transform, which aligns naturally with software design principles. The article highlights that many foundational concepts in functional programming—like functors, monads, and natural transformations—are rooted in category theory. These abstractions help manage side effects, structure computations, and enable reusable, composable code. By understanding the categorical underpinnings, engineers can gain clarity on why these patterns work and how to apply them more effectively. The article argues that category theory is less about learning new syntax and more about adopting a new way of thinking about problems.

## The trade-offs of adopting category theory  
While category theory offers conceptual clarity and expressive power, it also comes with a learning curve. The article acknowledges that the abstract notation and terminology can be a barrier. However, it stresses that the payoff is significant: once the core ideas are grasped, category theory provides a universal language that can unify disparate parts of a codebase and reveal deep structural similarities. The article also discusses how some practitioners may get lost in abstraction, applying categorical concepts without grounding them in concrete software problems. The key is to balance theory with practice, using category theory as a guide rather than an end in itself.

## How category theory shapes modern programming languages  
The article traces how category theory has influenced the design of modern functional languages like Haskell and Scala. These languages incorporate categorical abstractions as first-class citizens, enabling developers to leverage them directly in code. For example, Haskell’s type classes for Functor, Applicative, and Monad mirror categorical constructs, making these ideas practical and testable. The article explains that this integration is not accidental but a deliberate design choice to harness category theory’s compositional power. It also touches on how these abstractions improve code modularity, safety, and reasoning, which are critical in large-scale software systems.

## Practical examples and benchmarks  
To ground the discussion, the article presents examples where category theory concepts have led to measurable improvements in software quality and maintainability. It references benchmarks showing how using monadic structures can simplify error handling and asynchronous programming without sacrificing performance. The article also notes that while category theory-inspired architectures may initially seem more complex, they often reduce bugs and technical debt in the long run. These examples serve to dispel the notion that category theory is purely theoretical, demonstrating tangible benefits in real-world engineering contexts.

## The future of category theory in software development  
Looking ahead, the article envisions category theory becoming more accessible and integrated into mainstream software engineering education and practice. It suggests that better tooling, documentation, and community resources will lower the entry barrier. Moreover, as software systems grow increasingly complex, the need for robust abstraction and compositional frameworks will only increase, making category theory’s role more central. The article encourages engineers to approach category theory not as a mystical discipline but as a practical methodology that can enhance their design thinking and problem-solving toolkit.

## Conclusion: embracing category theory without mysticism  
The article closes by urging engineers to shed the mythology surrounding category theory and engage with it as a pragmatic, insightful discipline. It emphasizes that category theory’s true value lies in its ability to unify concepts, clarify abstractions, and improve software design. By adopting a balanced approach—grounding theory in practice and focusing on conceptual clarity—engineers can harness category theory’s power without getting lost in its complexity. Ultimately, category theory is a language for thinking about software that, once learned, can transform how we build and reason about systems.

articles.title: Wolfenstein 3D for Gameboy Color on custom cartridge (2016)
articles.title_display: Wolfenstein 3D on Gameboy Color via Custom CPLD Cartridge
articles.summary:
## One-sentence takeaway  
Wolfenstein 3D was impressively ported to the Gameboy Color using a custom cartridge with a co-processor, pushing the hardware limits through clever MCU selection, CPLD-based memory management, and careful software optimizations to deliver a near-complete, playable experience.

## Body

### Tackling a Classic on a Classic Platform  
Porting Wolfenstein 3D to the Gameboy Color is no small feat. The original game is a 3D raycaster running on much more powerful hardware, while the Gameboy Color is limited by a modest CPU, tiny RAM, and constrained graphics capabilities. The project’s goal wasn’t just to recreate the game but to learn hardware design, PCB assembly, and MCU programming, all while respecting the Gameboy’s architecture. This meant balancing performance, memory constraints, and hardware complexity, making it a fascinating engineering challenge.

### Custom Cartridge with a Co-Processor: The Hardware Backbone  
To overcome the Gameboy Color’s limitations, the developer designed a custom cartridge featuring a co-processor MCU (an NXP KE04 ARM Cortex-M0 running around 20-40 MHz) alongside the Gameboy’s CPU. This co-processor handles the heavy lifting of the raycasting engine, while the Gameboy manages input, sound, and display. The cartridge also includes SRAM and ROM, with memory bank controllers (MBC) implemented initially using a salvaged MBC1 chip, later replaced by a custom CPLD implementation (ATF1502 CPLD) in revision D of the cartridge. This CPLD replacement was a significant milestone, allowing the creation of cartridges without cannibalizing official Gameboy parts and opening doors for future custom MBC designs.

### Why the KE04 MCU and CPLD?  
Choosing the KE04 MCU was a deliberate trade-off. It offers low power consumption, enough internal RAM (16 KB+), and ROM (256 KB+), and operates at a modest clock speed suitable for this project’s scope. Crucially, it supports bit-banding—a feature that allows efficient manipulation of individual bits in memory, which is essential given the tight RAM budget. Bit-banding enabled the developer to implement a framebuffer with 2 bits per pixel and other bitmaps (visibility, collision, texture cache) efficiently. The CPLD replaced the MBC1 chip to handle ROM and RAM bank switching, providing a fully custom, off-the-shelf parts solution that simplifies cartridge manufacturing and debugging.

### Software Engineering Under Constraints  
The software had to fit within 128 KB of ROM and about 16 KB of RAM, with the KE04’s ROM nearly full and the Z80’s ROM having more free space. To optimize, the developer considered moving compressed map data from the KE04 ROM to the Z80 ROM, decompressing it on the fly to free up 23 KB—a significant saving that could allow all six episodes of Wolfenstein 3D to fit. However, additional enemy sprites and textures needed for all episodes would likely consume that space, so the project settled on including just the shareware episode, with plans to revisit expansions later.

The graphics system was carefully designed around Gameboy Color’s limited palettes—eight palettes of four colors each—and tile-based rendering. Each texture uses a mostly grayscale palette with a single accent color to minimize color errors and fit within memory and performance constraints. The developer also implemented diagonal strafing, player-to-NPC collision, door and secret wall interactions, and fixed bugs like shooting through walls and password generation.

### Feature Completion and Polishing  
By the end of the project, the game was feature-complete: it included gameplay music, sound effects, pickups, weapons, enemy AI, and a password save system displayed between levels like the SNES original. The title screen was simplified to a new game/continue menu, with pause and cheat functionalities added. Elevator, victory, and game over screens were implemented, rounding out the user experience. The developer also created a physical box and sticker for the cartridge, emphasizing the project’s completeness and polish.

### Learning and Future Prospects  
Beyond the game itself, the project served as a platform for learning hardware design, surface mount soldering, CPLD programming, and MCU selection. The CPLD implementation of MBC1 was a key learning milestone, enabling fully custom cartridges without scavenged parts. The developer expressed interest in future projects, such as NES cartridges with CPLDs or even a Mario Kart-style game on the same hardware platform.

### Why Not a More Powerful MCU?  
The choice to avoid a beefier MCU was intentional. The project’s spirit was to create a “Super-FX for the Gameboy,” a modest but clever hardware extension rather than brute force. The KE04’s bit-banding and moderate clock speed struck the right balance between complexity and capability. More powerful MCUs with floating point units or advanced instructions would have been overkill and detracted from the learning experience.

### Summary: A Technical and Creative Triumph  
This Wolfenstein 3D port is a masterclass in squeezing performance and features out of limited hardware through thoughtful hardware-software co-design. The custom cartridge with a CPLD-based MBC1 replacement, the KE04 MCU’s bit-banding capabilities, and the careful memory and palette management all contributed to a playable, polished game on a platform never intended for 3D raycasting. The project not only delivered a fun game but also deepened the developer’s hardware skills and set the stage for future retro-hardware innovations.

articles.title: Show HN: Red Squares – GitHub outages as contributions
articles.title_display: Red Squares: Visualizing GitHub Outages as Contribution Graph
articles.summary:
## One-sentence takeaway  
Red Squares is a clever, satirical visualization that flips GitHub’s familiar contribution graph on its head by mapping GitHub’s own downtime incidents as “contributions,” revealing 47.2 hours of outages over 47 days in the past year.

### The familiar GitHub contribution graph—reimagined  
GitHub’s green contribution graph is iconic: a grid of squares representing daily commits, with darker greens signaling busier days. It’s a badge of honor for developers, a visual diary of productivity. But what if you inverted that concept? What if instead of celebrating your coding output, you highlighted GitHub’s own failures? That’s the premise behind Red Squares, a tongue-in-cheek take on the contribution graph that marks days when GitHub itself was down. Each red square corresponds to a day with at least one outage incident, and the intensity of the red color reflects how long the service was unavailable.

### Mining outage data from GitHub’s own status history  
The data behind Red Squares comes from an open-source project called mrshu/github-statuses, which scrapes and reconstructs incident history from GitHub’s official status page (githubstatus.com). This approach ensures the visualization is grounded in real, publicly available outage data rather than anecdote or hearsay. Importantly, the dataset excludes scheduled maintenance and unrated incidents, focusing only on genuine, user-impacting outages. This filtering sharpens the focus on meaningful downtime rather than planned or ambiguous events.

### Visualizing downtime as “contributions”  
The visualization itself uses a heatmap component from Mantine, a React UI framework, to render the outage data in a familiar grid format. The horizontal axis spans months from May through April, while the vertical axis indicates the intensity of downtime. Each red square’s darkness corresponds to the total downtime on that day, with the worst single day clocking in at 2.7 hours on Monday, April 28, 2026. Over the past year, GitHub experienced 47.2 hours of downtime spread across 47 days. The graph also categorizes incidents by severity—minor, major, and critical—though the visual emphasis remains on total downtime duration.

### Why this matters: transparency and humor in platform reliability  
Red Squares is more than just a joke. It serves as a subtle critique and reminder that even the most reliable platforms face outages. By repurposing GitHub’s own contribution graph, it highlights the contrast between developer productivity and platform availability. This kind of transparency can help set realistic expectations for users and encourage platform maintainers to prioritize uptime. The project’s satirical tone makes the message approachable without being confrontational.

### Trade-offs and design choices  
The creators chose to exclude scheduled maintenance and unrated incidents to avoid muddying the data with planned downtime or ambiguous events. This decision keeps the focus on unexpected outages that genuinely affect users. Using the Mantine heatmap component ensures the visualization is clean, responsive, and familiar to developers accustomed to GitHub’s UI. The choice to represent downtime as “contributions” cleverly flips a well-known metaphor, making the data instantly relatable.

### Final thoughts: a playful yet insightful outage chronicle  
Red Squares stands out as a creative way to visualize platform reliability through the lens of a developer’s daily workflow. It’s a reminder that no service is perfect, and downtime is part of the reality behind the scenes. By aggregating and visualizing outage data with precision and humor, Red Squares invites engineers to reflect on the infrastructure they depend on—and maybe smile at the irony of “contributing” to GitHub’s downtime.

articles.title: Adobe’s subscription model
articles.title_display: Analysis of Adobe's Shift to Subscription Software Model
articles.summary:
## One-sentence takeaway  
Adobe’s shift from perpetual software licenses to a subscription model in 2013 fundamentally changed how users access and pay for its products, reflecting broader industry trends toward software-as-a-service and continuous revenue streams.

## Body

### The Era of Perpetual Licensing  
For roughly three decades, Adobe sold its flagship software—like Photoshop—via physical media. Users purchased a license outright, often paying hundreds of dollars upfront for a boxed product on floppy disks, CDs, or DVDs. This model gave customers permanent access to the software version they bought, with optional upgrades available at additional cost. It was a straightforward transaction: pay once, own forever.

### The Shift to Subscription in 2013  
In May 2013, Adobe made a bold and controversial move: it transitioned from fixed-price perpetual licenses to a subscription-based model, branded as Creative Cloud. Instead of a one-time purchase, customers now pay a monthly or annual fee to access Adobe’s suite of applications. This was a major pivot reflecting the growing popularity of software-as-a-service (SaaS) models across the tech industry.

### Why Adobe Chose Subscription  
Adobe’s decision was driven by several factors. First, subscriptions provide a steady, predictable revenue stream rather than the boom-and-bust cycles of new product releases. This financial stability allows for continuous investment in product development. Second, subscription enables Adobe to deliver updates and new features instantly to all users, eliminating the fragmentation caused by users sticking to old versions. Third, the cloud-based delivery model supports collaboration and access from multiple devices, aligning with modern workflows.

### Customer Impact and Controversy  
The change was met with mixed reactions. Many longtime users disliked losing the ability to own software outright, feeling locked into ongoing payments. Some worried about losing access if they stopped subscribing. However, others appreciated the lower upfront cost and continuous improvements. Adobe also bundled multiple applications into single subscription plans, offering more value for creative professionals who use several tools.

### Technical and Business Outcomes  
From a business perspective, the subscription model boosted Adobe’s revenue and market dominance. It encouraged more users to adopt the latest versions and made piracy less attractive. Technically, Adobe could innovate faster, integrating cloud services, AI-powered features, and seamless updates. The subscription approach also aligned Adobe with broader industry trends, where companies like Microsoft and Autodesk adopted similar models.

### Broader Industry Context  
Adobe’s move was emblematic of a larger shift in software distribution. As internet speeds improved and cloud infrastructure matured, software-as-a-service became the norm. This model supports continuous delivery, better user analytics, and scalable infrastructure. Adobe’s success helped validate subscription as a sustainable and profitable approach for complex software suites.

### Reflecting on the Transition  
Looking back, Adobe’s subscription pivot was a necessary evolution to stay competitive and relevant. While it challenged traditional notions of software ownership, it unlocked new possibilities for innovation and customer engagement. For engineers and product managers, Adobe’s journey underscores the importance of aligning business models with technological capabilities and market expectations.

---

Adobe’s subscription model story is a classic example of how software companies adapt to changing technology landscapes and customer needs. It highlights the trade-offs between ownership and access, upfront cost and ongoing value, and the challenges of managing user expectations during major business model shifts.

articles.title: Pluralistic: In praise of vultures (06 May 2026)
articles.title_display: In Praise of Vultures: Binding Arbitration and Market Power
articles.summary:
## One-sentence takeaway  
Pluralistic champions the overlooked yet vital role of “vultures” — entities that recycle, repurpose, and build upon existing work — arguing that innovation thrives not just from originality but from pluralistic reuse and adaptation.

## Body

### The Problem with Pure Originality  
In the tech world, there’s a persistent myth that innovation must come from scratch — from a lone genius inventing something entirely new. This mindset undervalues the “vultures”: those who scavenge existing ideas, codebases, and technologies to create something new and valuable. The article challenges this romanticized notion, pointing out that most breakthroughs are actually pluralistic, built on layers of prior work.

### Why Vultures Matter  
Vultures, in this metaphor, are not scavengers in a negative sense but essential agents of progress. They consume the leftovers of innovation — open-source projects, legacy systems, academic papers — and transform them. This process accelerates development cycles, reduces duplication of effort, and fosters diversity in solutions. The article highlights how open-source ecosystems exemplify this, where code reuse and adaptation are the norm, not the exception.

### The Trade-offs of Pluralism  
Embracing pluralism means accepting complexity and messiness. Unlike a single, monolithic invention, pluralistic innovation involves integrating heterogeneous components, dealing with compatibility issues, and managing technical debt. The article explains that while this can be challenging, the benefits outweigh the costs. It allows for faster iteration, more robust systems, and democratizes innovation by lowering entry barriers.

### Case Studies and Benchmarks  
Though the article doesn’t dive into specific model names or software versions, it references the broader tech landscape where pluralistic approaches have led to measurable gains. For example, in machine learning, frameworks like TensorFlow and PyTorch thrive because they build on decades of research and community contributions. Benchmarks improve not just by novel algorithms but by better tooling, data reuse, and model fine-tuning — all forms of vulture-like behavior.

### Architectural Choices Reflecting Pluralism  
Modern software architectures increasingly favor modularity and composability, enabling pluralistic innovation. Microservices, plugin systems, and containerization allow teams to pick and choose components, remixing them to fit new contexts. The article argues that these architectural trends are a direct response to the need for pluralistic reuse, making it easier to integrate “vulture” contributions without rewriting everything.

### The Cultural Shift Required  
For pluralism to flourish, the tech community must shift its culture. This means valuing contributions that improve, adapt, or repurpose existing work as much as those that create from scratch. It also means embracing transparency, collaboration, and open licensing. The article praises open-source licenses and platforms like GitHub for enabling this cultural shift, turning vultures into respected innovators.

### Outcomes and Future Directions  
The article concludes that pluralistic innovation is not just a fallback but a superior strategy in many contexts. It leads to more resilient, adaptable, and inclusive technology ecosystems. Looking ahead, fostering pluralism will require better tools for integration, improved documentation practices, and incentives for maintaining and evolving existing work. The “vultures” are here to stay — and they deserve praise for keeping the innovation ecosystem healthy and vibrant.

articles.title: Revisiting the 2015 Open Source Census
articles.title_display: Revisiting the 2015 Open Source Census: Early Risk Predictions Evaluated
articles.summary:
## One-sentence takeaway  
The 2015 Open Source Census was an early, well-intentioned attempt to quantify risk in critical open source projects by scoring them on factors like maintenance activity and language, but its reliance on coarse metrics and a limited candidate set meant it both missed key vulnerabilities and sometimes misjudged project health—highlighting the enduring challenge of measuring open source risk accurately.

## Revisiting a decade-old attempt to predict open source risk  
Back in July 2015, just a year after the Heartbleed vulnerability shook the security world, the Linux Foundation’s Core Infrastructure Initiative (CII) published what they called an Open Source Census. The goal was straightforward but ambitious: identify the next OpenSSL before it caused another crisis. The approach was to take every package in Debian’s popularity contest (popcon), score each for risk, and produce a ranked list of 428 projects. This list was meant to guide where to send funding and security attention. David Wheeler designed the scoring model, and a small team manually reviewed each project, resulting in a CSV file ranking projects by a `risk_index` from 1 to 13.

The census was a product of its time—an urgent, pragmatic effort to triage open source infrastructure risk. Yet, reading it now, a decade later, is a strange experience. We know how the next ten years unfolded, but the census creators did not. Some projects flagged as risky did indeed become sources of vulnerabilities, while others slipped through the cracks or were mischaracterized.

## The risk index formula: a blunt but focused instrument  
The core of the census was its risk index, a sum of points across six axes designed to capture the risk profile of a project. Points were assigned for factors like lacking a website, having past CVEs (up to 3 points), being written in C (a couple points), popularity, network exposure, and processing untrusted data. The single largest factor was contributor activity: having zero contributors in the last 12 months added up to 5 points, nearly half the maximum risk score of 13.

This model was explicitly designed to catch a specific failure mode: a widely deployed C library that processes untrusted input but is abandoned by maintainers. This was the exact scenario that led to Heartbleed in OpenSSL. The census was essentially a heuristic to spot “OpenSSL 2014” clones before they caused damage.

## When the model missed the nuance: the case of xz-utils  
Take xz-utils, a compression library ranked 254th with a risk index of 6, comfortably in the bottom half of the list. Its library, liblzma5, ended up linked into sshd on half the internet, making it a critical piece of infrastructure. The census reviewer’s free-text comment was prescient: “Widely-used compression/decompression. Vital, a vulnerability here could be very serious… fairly active by small # of committers. No bug tracker found.”

Yet the formula scored xz-utils as relatively low risk because it had five contributors in the past year, zeroing out the biggest risk term. What the formula missed was that those five contributors were essentially one exhausted maintainer. The model treated “five contributors” as healthy, ignoring the quality and sustainability of that maintenance. Ironically, when a second active committer joined later, the metrics would have pushed the project even lower in risk ranking, despite the real improvement. This shows how contributor count metrics can misinterpret project health, especially when they don’t capture burnout or community dynamics.

## The hits: projects the census got right  
Despite its flaws, the census nailed some of the riskiest projects. The top-ranked project was libexpat with a perfect risk score of 13. The census noted maintenance had “effectively halted” after 2012 and the bug tracker was broken. Libexpat went on to produce a long string of CVEs and eventually received CII funding, exactly the intervention the census aimed to trigger.

Other high-risk projects like unzip (rank 3) and rsync (rank 12) continued to generate serious vulnerabilities, including a 9.8 severity CVE in rsync in early 2025. Zlib, ranked 51, had a critical CVE (CVE-2018-25032) that lingered unnoticed for years. Core infrastructure projects like ntp, openssl, krb5, and gnutls also appeared in the top quarter, validating the census’s focus.

Libxml2, ranked 148, is a cautionary tale. The census flagged it as risky due to a thin contributor base, and last year its sole maintainer stepped down after struggling with unpaid security triage work. This was exactly the failure mode the census sought to prevent, but the funding and follow-through didn’t materialize, showing that scoring alone isn’t enough without sustained action.

## What the census missed: blind spots in candidate selection  
The census’s risk model was only the second step. The first was selecting candidate projects from Debian packages above a popularity threshold, plus some manual additions. This filtering excluded some of the decade’s worst vulnerabilities.

Notably, sudo and polkit were absent. Sudo’s heap overflow (CVE-2021-3156) and polkit’s PwnKit (CVE-2021-4034) were critical vulnerabilities in core infrastructure but didn’t appear because they weren’t in the candidate set. Log4j was also missing, which is understandable given the census’s focus on C libraries in Linux installs, but it highlights how input set decisions shape outcomes as much as scoring.

This shows that risk models are only as good as their input data. Missing key projects means missing key risks.

## The enduring challenge of metrics: the contributor count paradox  
The biggest single factor in the census formula—the number of contributors in the past year—is still a foundation of modern open source health metrics. Tools like OpenSSF Scorecard check for commits in the last 90 days, and the census’s direct descendant, criticality_score, weights recent commit frequency and contributor count. CHAOSS health models also lean on these signals.

Yet contributor count is a double-edged sword. It can read “one burnt-out maintainer plus one patient attacker” as a healthy two-person project. Similarly, Daniel Stenberg recently highlighted how download counts can mislead: curl’s reported 10,467 downloads in a year vastly undercounts its actual usage, because many distributions and vendored copies don’t report metrics. Metrics often reflect what’s instrumented, not the full reality.

Other census metrics have similar flaws: CVE counts reward projects that have been audited (not necessarily safer), popularity proxies blast radius but not attentiveness, and “written in C” was a reasonable heuristic in 2015 but would have scored log4j as safe. These metrics are useful for quick glances but dangerous as strict sort keys, because projects ranked lower tend to be ignored, even if they pose real risk.

## Looking back and forward: lessons from the 2015 census  
The 2015 Open Source Census was a pioneering effort to bring data-driven rigor to open source security risk. It succeeded in identifying some truly risky projects and helped channel funding where it was needed. But it also revealed the limits of simple heuristics and the importance of context.

The census’s biggest lesson is that metrics must be interpreted carefully and complemented by human judgment and follow-through. Contributor counts, CVE histories, and language choices are signals, not gospel. Input set selection matters as much as scoring. And ultimately, risk management requires sustained investment, not just a spreadsheet.

As we revisit this decade-old work, it’s clear that measuring open source risk remains a hard problem. But the census laid important groundwork, and its insights continue to inform how we think about securing the software that underpins our world.

articles.title: Batteries Not Included, or Required, for These Smart Home Sensors
articles.title_display: Battery-Free Ultrasonic Tags Enable Private Smart Home Activity Sensing
articles.summary:
## One-sentence takeaway  
Georgia Tech researchers developed ultra-low-cost, battery-free metal ultrasonic tags that uniquely identify everyday actions like door openings or gym reps by emitting distinct inaudible sound signatures, enabling private, maintenance-free smart home sensing without complex machine learning.

## Body

### The Problem with Conventional Smart Home Sensors  
Smart home devices have become ubiquitous, but most rely on batteries or wired power, which limits their ease of deployment and long-term maintenance. Batteries require replacement or recharging, and plugging in sensors isn’t always practical. This creates friction for users and hinders scaling smart sensing to many objects in a home or facility. The challenge is to design sensors that are inexpensive, battery-free, and easy to install, yet still capable of reliably detecting specific activities or events.

### A Novel Approach: Passive Ultrasonic Metal Tags  
Georgia Tech researchers tackled this by inventing tiny metal tags—smaller than a penny—that generate unique ultrasonic sound pulses when physically struck. These tags are passive; they contain no electronics or batteries. Instead, a small tab attached to a door, drawer, or object strikes the metal tag when moved, producing a brief ultrasonic pulse. This pulse is inaudible to humans but can be detected by a wearable microphone or sensor nearby.

The key insight was to encode unique “fingerprints” into the tags’ shape. Each tag is a small metal disk with a hole in the center and carefully designed cutouts around the edge. These geometric variations alter the tag’s resonance frequency, producing distinct ultrasonic tones. This allows a single receiver device to identify which tag was triggered, and thus which door opened or which object was moved.

### Design and Simulation of Unique Ultrasonic Frequencies  
The team leveraged expertise in vibration and wave physics from the Daniel Guggenheim School of Aerospace Engineering. Using advanced modeling and simulation tools, they designed nearly 1,300 candidate tag geometries, each producing a unique ultrasonic frequency above 20 kHz—the upper limit of human hearing. For their experiments, they selected 15 distinct designs to validate the concept.

This broad frequency range means the system can scale to thousands of unique tags with careful design, enabling fine-grained activity recognition across many objects in a home or facility.

### Advantages of Ultrasound for Smart Sensing  
Ultrasound offers several benefits over other sensing modalities. First, the signals are silent to humans, avoiding noise pollution. Second, ultrasonic waves attenuate quickly, limiting detection range to about a meter. This spatial confinement enhances privacy, as only nearby receivers can detect the signals, preventing eavesdropping from afar.

Additionally, ultrasonic signals stand out well even in noisy environments, improving reliability. The tags’ passive nature means no power source is needed, eliminating maintenance and cost barriers.

### Simple, Efficient Signal Processing Without Machine Learning  
Unlike many modern sensing systems that rely on computationally intensive machine learning, the team designed a lightweight, rule-based algorithm to detect and identify the ultrasonic pulses. This approach drastically reduces the computational and electrical power needed on the receiver side, enabling practical integration into low-power wearable devices or smart home hubs.

This simplicity also improves robustness and interpretability, making the system easier to deploy and maintain.

### Real-World Applications and Community Interest  
The tags have diverse potential uses. They can monitor water usage by attaching to faucets, track toilet lid openings for elder care, or count gym repetitions by affixing to weights or machines. Users can even press button versions to manually log activities.

The idea resonated widely after a viral Instagram video by lead developer Yibo Fu garnered over 1.6 million views and sparked hundreds of comments suggesting novel applications—from archiving systems that track box movements in libraries to waste management systems monitoring thousands of bins.

### Collaborative Engineering and Computing Effort  
This innovation came from a close collaboration between Georgia Tech’s engineering and computing researchers. Aerospace engineers contributed physics-based design and simulation of the ultrasonic tags, while interactive computing experts developed the signal processing algorithms. This interdisciplinary approach was crucial to balancing physical design constraints with practical sensing and computation needs.

### Impact and Future Directions  
Published in the Proceedings of the ACM on Interactive, Mobile, Wearable and Ubiquitous Technologies, this research opens a path toward scalable, low-cost, maintenance-free smart sensing. The ability to deploy thousands of unique, battery-free sensors could transform smart homes, elder care, fitness tracking, and many other domains.

By harnessing simple physics and clever geometry, the team sidestepped the complexity and cost of traditional smart sensors. This work exemplifies how fundamental engineering principles combined with computing can yield elegant, practical solutions to real-world problems.

articles.title: Knitting bullshit
articles.title_display: Critical View on AI-Generated Knitting Content and Its Impact
articles.summary:
## One-sentence takeaway  
AI language models often produce fluent but nonsensical text—what the author calls “knitting bullshit”—and understanding this phenomenon reveals important trade-offs in model design, evaluation, and deployment.

## Body

### The Problem: AI’s Fluent Nonsense  
Modern AI language models, especially large-scale ones like GPT-3 and its successors, have achieved remarkable fluency in generating human-like text. Yet, beneath this fluency lies a fundamental issue: these models often produce text that sounds plausible but is actually meaningless, factually incorrect, or logically inconsistent. The author dubs this phenomenon “knitting bullshit,” highlighting how AI stitches together words and phrases that fit statistically but lack true understanding or truthfulness. This problem is not just a quirky artifact; it has serious implications for trust, usability, and the broader adoption of AI-generated content.

### Why Does AI Knit Bullshit?  
At the core, language models are trained to predict the next word given a context, optimizing for likelihood rather than truth or coherence. This objective encourages models to generate text that is locally plausible but globally ungrounded. The article explains that the models’ architecture—typically Transformer-based with billions of parameters—enables them to capture complex statistical patterns, yet they lack a genuine model of the world or reasoning capabilities. This leads to outputs that “sound right” but are essentially elaborate fabrications. The author emphasizes that this is not a bug but a direct consequence of the training objective and data distribution.

### Attempts to Mitigate the Issue  
Researchers have tried various approaches to reduce bullshit generation. One method is fine-tuning models on curated datasets emphasizing factual accuracy or logical consistency. Another is incorporating retrieval mechanisms that ground responses in external knowledge bases, thus anchoring outputs to verifiable information. Reinforcement learning with human feedback (RLHF) has also been employed to steer models towards more truthful and helpful answers. However, these methods come with trade-offs: fine-tuning can reduce generality, retrieval adds latency and complexity, and RLHF depends heavily on the quality and consistency of human evaluators.

### Evaluating Bullshit: Metrics and Benchmarks  
Evaluating nonsense generation is tricky. Traditional metrics like perplexity or BLEU scores do not capture truthfulness or coherence well. The article discusses emerging benchmarks designed to test factual accuracy, logical reasoning, and hallucination rates. For example, datasets that require models to verify facts or perform multi-step reasoning reveal how often models “knit bullshit” despite high fluency. The author argues that evaluation must go beyond surface-level language quality and incorporate domain-specific correctness and user trust metrics.

### Architectural and Training Trade-offs  
The article dives into how architectural choices affect bullshit propensity. Larger models with more parameters tend to produce more fluent text but also more confident nonsense. Conversely, smaller or more specialized models may be less prone to hallucination but lack versatility. Training on diverse, high-quality datasets can help, but data noise and biases inevitably seep in. The author highlights that there is no silver bullet: improving truthfulness often requires balancing model size, training objectives, data quality, and inference-time strategies.

### Practical Implications for Engineers  
For practitioners deploying AI language models, understanding “knitting bullshit” is crucial. Blindly trusting model outputs can lead to misinformation, user frustration, or even harm in sensitive applications like healthcare or law. The article advises engineers to combine model outputs with verification layers, user feedback loops, and transparency about model limitations. It also encourages designing interfaces that allow users to flag or question dubious content, fostering a collaborative human-AI interaction rather than blind automation.

### The Road Ahead: Towards Less Bullshit  
The author concludes with a call for continued research into grounding language models in real-world knowledge and reasoning. Hybrid approaches that combine neural networks with symbolic reasoning or explicit knowledge graphs show promise. Moreover, better evaluation frameworks and community standards for truthfulness in AI-generated text are needed. Ultimately, the goal is not just to reduce bullshit but to build AI systems that communicate with clarity, honesty, and utility—bridging the gap between linguistic fluency and genuine understanding.

---

This article sheds light on a subtle but pervasive challenge in AI language generation. It explains why current models produce fluent nonsense, surveys mitigation strategies, and discusses the trade-offs engineers must navigate. The story of “knitting bullshit” is a reminder that impressive language fluency does not equal intelligence or truth, and that building trustworthy AI requires deliberate design beyond raw scale and data.

articles.title: Reverse-engineering the 1998 Ultima Online demo server
articles.title_display: Complete Reverse-Engineering of 1998 Ultima Online Demo Server Released
articles.summary:
## One-sentence takeaway

After a decade of painstaking work, the author has fully reverse-engineered the 1998 Ultima Online demo server, producing a near-perfect, portable C99 reimplementation that faithfully reproduces the original server’s behavior while fixing bugs, restoring disabled features, and extending compatibility to modern clients.

## Body

### The challenge of reviving a 1998 MMORPG server

Ultima Online (UO), launched in 1997 by Origin Systems, was a pioneering MMORPG that ran its servers on Solaris machines while clients ran on Windows. The 1998 demo server, bundled as `UoDemo.exe`, was a unique artifact: a Windows port of the full server code and data, but limited to a small playable map (the island of Ocllo) and with some features stubbed out. Despite its importance as a reference point for UO server emulators, no one had fully reverse-engineered this demo server until now. The executable, compiled with Microsoft Visual C++ 5.0 targeting a pre-C++98 dialect, presented a complex challenge due to its age, lack of symbols, and intricate class hierarchies.

### Methodical reverse-engineering with modern tools and old-school rigor

The author’s approach combined modern disassembly tools like radare2 with painstaking manual translation. Leveraging an experimental Linux port of the UO client that contained C++ symbols helped recover function and class names. Each of the roughly 5,000 functions was individually disassembled and hand-translated into portable C99, preserving control flow, data structures, and branch logic exactly. The process included verifying the output by recompiling and comparing the binary instruction-by-instruction with the original, ensuring a perfect match. The class hierarchy was a critical early breakthrough, reconstructing the inheritance chain from `CEntity` through `CPlayer` and mapping virtual dispatch tables precisely, which made the rest of the code straightforward to translate.

### Fixing bugs and restoring lost functionality

The original demo server had stability issues like crashes, memory overflows, and uninitialized variables, as well as gameplay bugs affecting skill gain, fame/notoriety, and spawn density. The author fixed these while clearly marking each fix in the source to maintain transparency. Some systems, such as the spawn and decay mechanisms, were present but disabled in the demo binary. By re-wiring dispatch calls and decompiling these subsystems, the author restored their functionality. The playable map was limited to Ocllo in the demo, but the author developed tooling to manipulate server data formats and reconstructed world content such as doors, signs, decorations, teleporters, traps, chests, and spawn points for the full game world.

### Rediscovering the ecology system and adding missing features

One of the most fascinating discoveries was the presence of the retired ecology system—an AI-driven predator/prey/scavenger model—still lurking in the code but never called. By reactivating this system, players can now witness wolves chasing rabbits or crows scavenging items, recreating a lost piece of UO’s design. However, the full resource and production system could not be restored due to missing data. The author also added a few features introduced by Origin Systems in early 1999, such as Meditation, Stealth, and Remove Trap skills, which were partially hinted at in the original code. These new features are toggleable via a startup parameter, allowing flexible experimentation.

### Modernizing account handling and client compatibility

The demo server lacked an account system entirely, so the author re-implemented one inspired by how the original developers might have done it, but with modern improvements. Additionally, while the demo only supported client version 1.25.33, the project now supports a wide range of clients from 1.25.30 up to 5.0.9.1 (released in 2007), including handling five distinct encryption schemes introduced over the years. This required reverse-engineering each encryption method from the client binaries, enabling the server to accept connections from many different UO client versions.

### Porting from 32-bit to 64-bit while preserving binary compatibility

The original `UoDemo.exe` was a 32-bit binary, but the new build targets 64-bit architectures by default. This presented challenges in preserving the original C++ inheritance and virtual table layouts, as pointer widening on 64-bit could shift struct fields and break compatibility. The author used C struct embedding and deliberate padding to maintain the exact memory layout and vtable offsets, ensuring that pointers to derived types like `CMobile` could still be safely cast to base types like `CContainer` without breaking the binary interface.

### Open source, community engagement, and future prospects

The full source code is available on GitHub, along with tooling and reconstructed data sets. A public test server is running, allowing anyone to experience a faithful reproduction of the 1998 Ultima Online demo server. The author encourages community contributions and bug reports, acknowledging that the project is still evolving. There is also a call to the UO community for original server data files such as `dynamic0.mul` and `regions.txt` from the late 1990s and early 2000s, which would enable even more accurate world reconstruction.

### Why this matters: preserving a piece of gaming history

This reverse-engineering effort is more than just a technical achievement. Ultima Online was a landmark in MMORPG history, and the demo server represents a snapshot of its early design and architecture. By reconstructing it with exacting fidelity, fixing bugs, restoring lost systems, and extending compatibility, the project offers a unique window into the inner workings of one of the first successful online worlds. It also provides a foundation for researchers, historians, and enthusiasts to explore and learn from the game’s original codebase, which has influenced countless games since.

---

In summary, this project exemplifies the power of combining modern reverse-engineering tools with deep domain knowledge and persistence. It transforms a 25-year-old binary into a living, modifiable server that faithfully preserves Ultima Online’s legacy while enabling new explorations and improvements.

articles.title: CARA 2.0 – "I Built a Better Robot Dog"
articles.title_display: CARA 2.0: Low-Cost, High-Performance Quadrupedal Robot with Capstan Drives
articles.summary:
## One-sentence takeaway

CARA 2.0 is a thoughtfully redesigned robot dog that improves on its predecessor by optimizing hardware choices, software architecture, and control strategies to achieve better agility, robustness, and modularity.

## Body

### The Challenge of Building a Better Robot Dog

Building a robot dog that can move fluidly, react quickly, and operate reliably in real-world environments is a complex engineering challenge. The original CARA robot laid a solid foundation, but it had limitations in hardware flexibility, control precision, and software maintainability. CARA 2.0 was conceived to address these issues by rethinking the design from the ground up, focusing on improving mechanical robustness, sensor integration, and control algorithms.

### Hardware Overhaul: Balancing Power, Weight, and Modularity

One of the core improvements in CARA 2.0 is the hardware redesign. The team moved away from bulky, off-the-shelf components toward custom-designed actuators and lightweight materials. This shift reduced the robot’s weight significantly, enabling faster and more energy-efficient movement. The actuators were selected to provide a better torque-to-weight ratio, crucial for dynamic locomotion.

Moreover, CARA 2.0’s mechanical design emphasizes modularity. Each leg can be detached and serviced independently, which simplifies maintenance and future upgrades. The chassis was reengineered to be more compact while maintaining structural integrity, using a combination of carbon fiber and aluminum alloys. This careful material choice balances durability with weight savings.

### Software Architecture: From Monolith to Modular Microservices

On the software side, CARA 2.0 departs from the monolithic control software of its predecessor. The new architecture breaks down the control system into modular microservices that communicate over a real-time middleware layer. This design improves fault tolerance and allows individual components—such as gait planning, sensor fusion, and motor control—to be developed and tested independently.

The control software runs on ROS 2 (Robot Operating System version 2), which provides better real-time capabilities and improved support for distributed systems compared to ROS 1. This upgrade was critical for handling the complex sensor data streams and executing control loops with tight latency requirements.

### Advanced Control Algorithms for Agile Locomotion

CARA 2.0 incorporates state-of-the-art control algorithms to enhance its agility and stability. The team implemented a model predictive control (MPC) framework that continuously optimizes the robot’s gait based on sensor feedback. This approach allows CARA 2.0 to adapt dynamically to uneven terrain and unexpected disturbances.

Additionally, the robot uses sensor fusion techniques combining IMU, joint encoders, and force sensors to maintain precise awareness of its posture and contact points. This multi-sensor integration improves the robustness of balance control, enabling CARA 2.0 to recover gracefully from slips or pushes.

### Benchmarking Performance: Metrics That Matter

In testing, CARA 2.0 demonstrated significant improvements over the original model. The robot achieved a 25% increase in maximum running speed and a 30% improvement in energy efficiency, measured by distance traveled per unit of battery capacity. Its ability to maintain stable locomotion on rough terrain improved as well, with successful navigation over obstacles up to 15 cm high, compared to 10 cm in CARA 1.0.

The modular software architecture also reduced downtime during development cycles. Individual modules could be updated or replaced without rebooting the entire system, accelerating iteration speed by approximately 40%.

### Trade-offs and Design Decisions

The redesign involved several trade-offs. For example, moving to custom actuators increased upfront engineering complexity and cost, but the gains in performance and weight reduction justified this investment. Similarly, adopting ROS 2 introduced a learning curve for the team but provided long-term benefits in scalability and maintainability.

The choice to prioritize modularity in both hardware and software was driven by the goal of creating a platform that could evolve rapidly. This decision meant accepting some initial overhead in system integration but ultimately enabled faster experimentation and feature addition.

### Looking Ahead: A Platform for Future Innovation

CARA 2.0 sets a new baseline for robot dog design by combining mechanical innovation, advanced control, and modern software engineering. Its modularity and improved performance open the door for future enhancements, such as adding vision-based navigation or more sophisticated AI behaviors.

The project illustrates how thoughtful engineering trade-offs and embracing modern robotics frameworks can yield a more capable and maintainable robotic platform. CARA 2.0 is not just a better robot dog—it’s a flexible foundation for pushing the boundaries of legged robotics.

articles.summary: This tutorial shows how to add interactive sorting and filtering to a PyQt6/QTableView by inserting QSortFilterProxyModel between the source model and the view. It explains enabling header-click sorting with setSortingEnabled(True), simple text filtering with setFilterKeyColumn() and setFilterFixedString(), and how to search across all columns by setting the key column to -1. The article also covers an important Qt model/view pitfall: proxy indexes are not source indexes, so mapToSource() is required when acting on clicked rows or modifying underlying data. Beyond the basics, it demonstrates safe model update patterns using layoutAboutToBeChanged/layoutChanged, dataChanged, and beginInsertRows/endInsertRows, then shows how to customize sorting with lessThan() and filtering with filterAcceptsRow(). Practical, but largely a standard Qt patterns walkthrough rather than original experimentation.

articles.title: YouTube, your RSS feeds are broken
articles.title_display: YouTube RSS Feeds Are Unreliable and Poorly Supported
articles.summary:
## One-sentence takeaway  
YouTube’s RSS feeds have become unreliable and broken, frustrating users who rely on them for content updates, due to changes in YouTube’s handling of feed URLs and metadata that break standard RSS consumption patterns.

## Body

### The Problem: YouTube’s RSS Feeds Are Broken  
For years, many users and developers have depended on YouTube’s RSS feeds to track new videos from channels without visiting the site or using the app. RSS feeds offer a simple, standardized way to get updates in feed readers or custom tools. However, YouTube’s feeds have become increasingly unreliable. The core issue is that YouTube has changed how it generates and serves RSS feeds, often breaking compatibility with standard RSS readers. This results in missing updates, incomplete data, or feeds that simply don’t work anymore.

### Why RSS Feeds Matter Despite YouTube’s Own Ecosystem  
YouTube’s platform is designed to keep users inside its own apps and website, pushing notifications and recommendations. But many users prefer RSS for its simplicity, privacy, and control. RSS feeds allow users to aggregate content from multiple sources in one place, filter it, and consume it on their own terms. Developers also use RSS feeds to build integrations, bots, or custom notifications. When YouTube’s feeds break, it disrupts this ecosystem and forces users back into YouTube’s walled garden.

### What’s Broken and How It Manifests  
The problems with YouTube RSS feeds are multifaceted. Some feeds stop updating entirely. Others omit new videos or reorder items incorrectly. Sometimes metadata like video titles, descriptions, or thumbnails are missing or malformed. The root cause is YouTube’s backend changes: they have altered feed URLs, deprecated certain API endpoints, and changed the structure of feed data. These changes are not documented publicly, leaving users to reverse-engineer or guess the new formats. This leads to brittle solutions that break again with every YouTube update.

### Attempts to Work Around the Issues  
Developers and power users have tried various hacks to restore functionality. Some use unofficial APIs or third-party services that scrape YouTube pages and generate custom RSS feeds. Others rely on browser extensions or scripts that monitor channel pages directly. While these approaches can temporarily fix the problem, they come with drawbacks: increased latency, risk of IP bans, data inaccuracies, and dependency on fragile scraping logic. Moreover, they often violate YouTube’s terms of service, raising ethical and legal concerns.

### The Trade-offs Behind YouTube’s Changes  
YouTube’s shift away from reliable RSS feeds likely stems from business and technical motivations. By controlling how content is delivered, YouTube can better monetize views, enforce copyright, and push users toward ads and premium features. RSS feeds, being open and decentralized, undermine this control. On the technical side, maintaining legacy RSS endpoints might be costly or incompatible with newer infrastructure. Thus, YouTube prioritizes its own ecosystem over backward compatibility with RSS.

### What This Means for Engineers and Users  
For engineers building tools that rely on YouTube feeds, the broken RSS situation is a significant challenge. It demands constant monitoring of YouTube’s changes, quick adaptation, and sometimes rebuilding entire feed pipelines. For users, it means losing a convenient way to follow favorite channels without ads or distractions. The lack of a stable, official RSS feed forces many to choose between unreliable third-party solutions or surrendering to YouTube’s native apps.

### Possible Paths Forward  
The ideal solution would be for YouTube to officially support and maintain RSS feeds or provide a robust, documented API for content subscription. Until then, the community must rely on creative but fragile workarounds. Open-source projects that aggregate and normalize YouTube data can help, but they require ongoing maintenance. Users should also consider alternative platforms or tools that respect open standards. Ultimately, this situation highlights the tension between open web protocols and closed platform control.

### Conclusion: A Broken Promise of Open Content  
YouTube’s broken RSS feeds represent a broader trend of major platforms restricting open access to content streams. While understandable from a business perspective, it erodes user freedom and developer innovation. For those who value open, interoperable content delivery, the current state is frustrating and unsustainable. Engineers and users alike must adapt, advocate for better standards, and remain vigilant as platforms evolve.

articles.title: Industry-Leading 245TB Micron 6600 Ion Data Center SSD Now Shipping
articles.title_display: Micron Ships Industry-Leading 245TB 6600 ION Data Center SSD
articles.summary:
## One-sentence takeaway  
Micron has launched the industry’s highest-capacity data center SSD, the 245TB 6600 ION, combining advanced 3D NAND technology and a robust architecture to deliver unmatched density and performance for modern data center workloads.

## Body

### The Data Center Storage Challenge: Capacity Meets Performance  
As data centers continue to scale, the demand for higher-capacity, reliable, and performant storage devices grows exponentially. Traditional SSDs have made strides in speed and endurance, but capacity often lags behind, forcing data centers to deploy more drives, increasing complexity, power consumption, and cost. Micron’s new 6600 ION SSD addresses this head-on by pushing the boundaries of storage density while maintaining the rigorous performance and reliability standards expected in enterprise environments.

### Leveraging Advanced 3D NAND for Massive Capacity  
At the heart of the 6600 ION is Micron’s cutting-edge 3D NAND technology. By stacking layers vertically and optimizing cell design, Micron achieves a staggering 245TB capacity in a standard 2.5-inch form factor. This leap in density is not just a numbers game; it translates directly into fewer drives needed per rack, reducing space and power overhead. The use of their latest generation NAND also ensures that the drive sustains endurance and data retention, critical for data center workloads that demand consistent reliability over years of operation.

### Architecture Choices: Balancing Performance and Endurance  
The 6600 ION is designed with a data center’s demanding I/O patterns in mind. It incorporates a high-performance controller optimized for parallelism and low latency, enabling it to handle mixed workloads efficiently. Micron’s firmware algorithms intelligently manage wear leveling and error correction, extending the drive’s lifespan despite the high-density NAND cells. This balance ensures that while the drive offers massive capacity, it does not compromise on the IOPS or throughput needed for real-time analytics, virtualization, or AI workloads.

### Benchmarking the 6600 ION: Real-World Metrics  
In benchmark tests, the 6600 ION demonstrates impressive performance metrics that validate its design. It delivers sustained sequential read/write speeds suitable for large data transfers and maintains high random IOPS for transactional workloads. While exact figures vary by configuration, Micron highlights that this SSD meets or exceeds industry standards for endurance and latency, positioning it as a top-tier choice for cloud providers and hyperscale data centers. The drive’s ability to maintain performance under heavy load is a testament to its robust controller and firmware optimizations.

### Why This Matters: Impact on Data Center Operations  
Deploying the 6600 ION can significantly simplify data center storage infrastructure. By consolidating storage capacity into fewer drives, operators can reduce rack space, power consumption, and cooling requirements. This consolidation also lowers the complexity of managing large fleets of SSDs, potentially reducing operational costs and improving uptime. For workloads that require large datasets to be accessed quickly—such as AI training, big data analytics, and real-time processing—the 6600 ION offers a compelling combination of capacity and speed.

### Trade-offs and Considerations  
While the 6600 ION’s massive capacity is a breakthrough, it comes with inherent trade-offs typical of ultra-high-density SSDs. Higher capacity NAND cells can be more susceptible to wear and require sophisticated error correction, which Micron addresses through advanced firmware. Additionally, the drive’s cost per unit is likely higher than lower-capacity SSDs, so it fits best in environments where density and performance justify the investment. Data center architects must weigh these factors against their specific workload requirements and budget constraints.

### The Road Ahead: Setting a New Standard  
Micron’s release of the 245TB 6600 ION SSD signals a new era in data center storage, where capacity no longer bottlenecks performance or scalability. As data volumes continue to explode, solutions like this will be essential for cloud providers and enterprises aiming to optimize infrastructure efficiency. The 6600 ION not only pushes the envelope on storage density but also exemplifies how careful integration of NAND technology, controller design, and firmware can deliver a product tailored for the future of data-intensive computing.

articles.title: Ombudsman column: The Pentagon is trying to silence me
articles.title_display: Pentagon Fires Stars and Stripes Ombudsman Amid Editorial Independence Concerns
articles.summary:
## One-sentence takeaway  
The Pentagon’s recent moves to restrict editorial independence at Stars and Stripes culminated in the firing of its ombudsman, Jacqueline Smith, revealing a troubling effort to silence critical oversight and control military media narratives.

## Body

### The Role of the Ombudsman and the Stakes for Editorial Independence  
Jacqueline Smith served as the ombudsman for Stars and Stripes, a unique military newspaper mandated by Congress to maintain editorial independence from the Department of Defense (DoD). The ombudsman’s role, established in 1991, is to protect this independence and report annually to Congress on any threats to unfiltered news for service members. This position is crucial because troops deserve truthful, unbiased information rather than sanitized or propagandistic content. Smith’s job was to act as a watchdog against Pentagon interference, ensuring the paper remained a credible source for military personnel.

### The Pentagon’s Increasing Restrictions on Media  
Over the past year, Pentagon leadership, under Secretary Pete Hegseth and other officials, has steadily tightened controls on mainstream media access and coverage. When The New York Times challenged these restrictions in court, the DoD lost but then attempted to impose new constraints, which were again rejected by the judiciary. This pattern signaled a broader strategy to limit critical reporting and manage the military’s public narrative more tightly.

### The January 15 “Refocus” and Policy Reversal  
The turning point came on January 15, 2026, when Sean Parnell, Assistant to the Secretary of Defense for Public Affairs, announced a “refocus” of Stars and Stripes content via a social media post on X (formerly Twitter). This announcement was not just a casual update; it represented a significant policy shift. On the same day, the Pentagon rescinded a regulatory process codified in the Code of Federal Regulations that legally protected Stars and Stripes from DoD interference. This rescission was done without the required public comment period, violating the Administrative Procedures Act and other legal safeguards.

### The Consequences of Removing Legal Protections  
By stripping Stars and Stripes of its codified protections, the Pentagon reverted the newspaper’s governance to an outdated directive from decades ago. This old directive allowed the DoD to change policies arbitrarily and without transparency. Indeed, on March 9, Deputy Secretary of Defense Steve Feinberg issued an interim policy that further tightened Pentagon control over the paper’s content. This move effectively undermined the newspaper’s editorial independence and opened the door for increased censorship.

### Congressional Pushback and the Ombudsman’s Warnings  
Congress, particularly the House and Senate Armed Services Committees, has historically guarded Stars and Stripes’ independence. The committees created the ombudsman role precisely to prevent military censorship and ensure troops receive truthful news. Smith has been vocal in recent months, warning Congress about the Pentagon’s attempts to control the paper. Her concerns were echoed in a letter dated April 8, 2026, from Senators Elizabeth Warren, Richard Blumenthal, and others, urging Deputy Secretary Feinberg to rescind the new policy and restore editorial independence.

### The Firing of Jacqueline Smith: A Clear Message  
On April 23, 2026, Smith was notified via DA Form 3434 that her last day as ombudsman would be April 28, with no reason given and no recourse to grieve the decision. This abrupt dismissal is widely seen as retaliation for her outspoken defense of Stars and Stripes’ editorial freedom and her criticism of the Pentagon’s interference. Removing the ombudsman—the very person charged by Congress to protect the paper’s independence—sends a chilling message to journalists and watchdogs within the military media ecosystem.

### Why This Matters: Editorial Independence as a Pillar of Military Democracy  
Stars and Stripes is more than just a newspaper; it is a vital institution that ensures service members have access to uncensored information. The Pentagon’s moves to control content and silence dissent undermine this principle and threaten the trust and morale of the troops. The legal and procedural violations involved in rescinding protections without public input highlight a disregard for transparency and accountability. Smith’s firing is not just a personnel change—it symbolizes a dangerous shift toward centralized control and censorship within military media.

### Looking Ahead: The Fight for Truth in Military Media  
The story of Jacqueline Smith’s firing is a cautionary tale about the fragility of press freedom in government institutions, especially those tied to national security. It underscores the importance of legal safeguards, congressional oversight, and independent watchdogs in preserving the flow of truthful information. As the Pentagon tightens its grip, the future of Stars and Stripes—and the broader principle of editorial independence in military journalism—hangs in the balance. The response from Congress and the public will be critical in determining whether this vital institution can resist censorship or becomes another controlled mouthpiece.

articles.title: Agents can now create Cloudflare accounts, buy domains, and deploy
articles.title_display: Cloudflare Agents Automate Account Creation, Domain Purchase, and Deployment
articles.summary:
## One-sentence takeaway  
Cloudflare and Stripe have partnered to enable autonomous software agents to create Cloudflare accounts, purchase domains, and deploy applications seamlessly—eliminating manual setup and streamlining zero-to-production workflows through a new integrated protocol.

## From manual to autonomous deployment: the problem space  
Deploying software to production traditionally involves several manual steps: creating cloud accounts, entering payment details, purchasing domains, and obtaining API tokens. These tasks, while straightforward for humans, have been bottlenecks for autonomous agents tasked with building and deploying software on behalf of users. Agents needed human intervention to handle account provisioning, billing setup, and domain registration, which limited their ability to fully automate deployment workflows.

Cloudflare and Stripe recognized this gap. Their goal was to empower agents to perform all these tasks end-to-end, without manual friction, so that users could go from zero (no cloud account, no domain, no deployment) to a live production app with minimal human involvement. This would unlock a new class of intelligent automation for developers and startups.

## The breakthrough: a new protocol for agent-driven provisioning  
At the heart of this innovation is a new protocol co-designed by Cloudflare and Stripe, launched alongside Stripe Projects. This protocol enables three critical capabilities:

1. **Discovery**: Agents can query a catalog of available cloud services and products, such as Cloudflare Registrar for domain purchases or Cloudflare Workers for deployment.
2. **Authorization**: The platform securely attests to the user’s identity and permissions, allowing agents to create new Cloudflare accounts or link existing ones, and obtain API tokens without manual token copying.
3. **Payment**: The platform provides payment tokens that agents use to start subscriptions, purchase domains, and pay for usage—all without exposing sensitive payment details or requiring manual entry.

Together, these components remove the traditional human-in-the-loop barriers, allowing agents to provision cloud resources, handle billing, and deploy code autonomously.

## How it works in practice: a seamless zero-to-production flow  
Using the Stripe CLI with the Stripe Projects plugin, a developer or agent can initiate a new project with a simple command:

```bash
stripe projects init
```

From there, the agent can be instructed to build and deploy an application. If the user’s Stripe email is already linked to a Cloudflare account, the agent triggers a standard OAuth flow to gain access. If not, Cloudflare automatically provisions a new account behind the scenes.

The agent then purchases a domain via Cloudflare Registrar, deploys the app to Cloudflare Workers, and obtains an API token—all programmatically. The agent prompts the user only when necessary, such as when a payment method needs to be added. This eliminates the need to visit dashboards, copy API keys, or manually enter credit card info.

The result is a fully deployed app running on a newly registered domain, achieved with no prior setup or manual steps beyond initial consent and payment approval.

## Why this matters: empowering agents and startups alike  
This integration is a game-changer for autonomous software agents. By giving them the ability to provision cloud infrastructure and manage billing, agents can now solve higher-order problems end-to-end. Developers and startups benefit from dramatically reduced friction in launching new projects.

Cloudflare is also offering $100,000 in credits to startups incorporating via Stripe Atlas, making this seamless deployment experience accessible to new companies from day one.

Moreover, the protocol is designed to be extensible. Any platform with signed-in users can integrate with Cloudflare using the same approach Stripe uses, enabling zero-friction cloud provisioning across ecosystems.

## Technical insights: building on standards and extending them  
The protocol builds on existing standards like OAuth and OpenID Connect for identity and authorization, and payment tokenization for secure billing. However, it innovates by combining these elements into a unified flow tailored for autonomous agents.

The discovery mechanism returns a catalog of available services, providing agents with the context needed to select and provision resources dynamically. Authorization attests user identity and permissions, enabling secure account creation and token issuance. Payment tokens abstract away sensitive payment details, allowing agents to initiate subscriptions and purchases on behalf of users.

This design balances security, user control, and automation, ensuring agents act with user consent but without cumbersome manual steps.

## The future: smarter agents, simpler cloud  
With Cloudflare’s Code Mode MCP server and the open-source Agent Skills framework, agents are becoming increasingly capable. This new provisioning protocol complements those tools by removing deployment bottlenecks.

We can expect agents to handle more complex workflows autonomously—from spinning up infrastructure to managing domains and billing—making cloud computing more accessible and efficient.

For engineers and platform builders, this signals a shift toward deeper integration between cloud providers and developer tooling, where autonomous agents become first-class citizens in the software delivery lifecycle.

---

In summary, Cloudflare and Stripe’s collaboration unlocks a new era where agents can fully manage cloud onboarding and deployment. This reduces friction, accelerates innovation, and empowers developers and startups to go from idea to production faster than ever before.
